agent_output: {"summary":{"verdict":"BLOCK","one_liner":"1-indexed pagination contract is violated — page 1 skips the first pageSize rows, silently returning wrong data to every caller."},"critical_findings":[{"title":"Off-by-one: page=1 skips first pageSize results","risk":"Every API consumer requesting page 1 gets page 2's data. The first page of orders is unreachable. This is silent data corruption — no error, just wrong results.","severity":"Critical","location":"getOrderHistory, line: const offset = page * pageSize","reproduction":"Call getOrderHistory('cust-1', 1, 10). offset = 1*10 = 10. The query skips the 10 most recent orders. Page 1 returns what should be page 2. Page 0 (undocumented) returns the real first page.","fix":"Change to: const offset = (page - 1) * pageSize;","time_to_fix":"1 minute","acceptance_criteria_affected":"API contract states page=1 is first page; current implementation violates this"},{"title":"No input validation allows negative offset or zero/negative LIMIT","risk":"page=0 or page=-5 produces negative or zero offsets. pageSize=0 returns nothing. pageSize=-1 may cause undefined DB behavior or return all rows depending on the driver. An attacker or buggy client can abuse this.","severity":"High","location":"getOrderHistory, top of function (missing guards)","reproduction":"Call getOrderHistory('cust-1', -1, 10) → offset = -10. Behavior is DB-dependent: Postgres will error, other drivers may not. Call with pageSize=0 → always returns []. Call with pageSize=999999 → uncontrolled memory/bandwidth consumption.","fix":"Add guards: if (page < 1) throw new Error('page must be >= 1'); if (pageSize < 1 || pageSize > MAX_PAGE_SIZE) throw new Error('invalid pageSize'); Also validate customerId is non-empty.","time_to_fix":"5 minutes","acceptance_criteria_affected":"API robustness and DoS resistance"},{"title":"OFFSET pagination degrades to full table scan on deep pages","risk":"OFFSET N forces Postgres to scan and discard N rows before returning results. For a customer with 1M orders, page 100000 scans ~1M rows. This is a latency bomb and can saturate DB CPU under normal usage patterns like automated crawlers or export jobs.","severity":"High","location":"SQL query: OFFSET $3","reproduction":"Insert 500k orders for a customer. Request page=50000&pageSize=10. Observe query time growing linearly with page number vs. constant for keyset pagination.","fix":"Switch to keyset (cursor) pagination: WHERE created_at < $cursor ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT $2. Return the last row's created_at as the next cursor. If OFFSET must be kept for backward compat, add a max page depth guard (e.g., page * pageSize <= 10000).","time_to_fix":"30 minutes for keyset, 5 minutes for depth guard","acceptance_criteria_affected":"Performance SLA under realistic data volumes"}],"important_issues":[{"title":"SELECT * returns all columns including potentially large/sensitive fields","description":"SELECT * pulls every column (e.g., internal notes, PII, large JSON blobs). This wastes bandwidth and may leak data not intended for the API response. Explicitly list needed columns.","location":"SQL query: SELECT *"},{"title":"No total count or hasNextPage metadata","description":"Pagination without total count or a next-page indicator forces clients to guess when to stop paginating, leading to wasted requests or incomplete data display.","location":"Return type Promise<Order[]>"}],"suggestions":["Add an index on (customer_id, created_at DESC) if one doesn't exist — without it, every page request is a sequential scan filtered by customer_id","Consider returning { data: Order[], nextCursor: string } for cursor-based pagination","Add request-level timeout or statement_timeout to guard against pathologically slow deep-page queries"],"production_readiness":{"deployment_risks":"The off-by-one bug means every caller already in production is getting wrong data. Fixing it changes which rows are returned for every page number, so clients caching old page results will see inconsistencies during rollout.","rollback_capability":"Rollback is safe — reverting returns to the current (broken) behavior. No schema changes involved.","backward_compatibility":"Fixing the offset changes the API's observable behavior. If any client has already compensated for the bug (e.g., requesting page 0), the fix will break them. Coordinate with consumers.","monitoring_coverage":"No monitoring mentioned. Add: query latency by page depth, empty-result-set rate (signals off-by-one), and error rate on validation failures.","breaking_changes":{"api_breaking":true,"db_destructive":false,"shared_library":false,"auth_security":false,"data_pipeline":false,"config_changes":false,"details":["Fixing the offset changes which rows are returned for every page number — any client relying on current (broken) behavior will see different results"]},"safe_to_deploy":false,"rollback_plan":"Revert the function to its previous state. No migrations or state changes to undo."},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":97,"high_confidence_areas":["Off-by-one is deterministic and trivially reproducible","Input validation gap is clear from code inspection","OFFSET performance characteristics are well-documented Postgres behavior"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact DB driver behavior for negative LIMIT/OFFSET depends on which driver is in use"],"assumptions":["Database is Postgres (based on $1 parameterized query syntax)","No application-layer middleware is adjusting page numbers before this function is called"]}}
